body:
## Problem
Game runs only in a browser tab. No native window,
no file system access for save/load, no install experience.

## Solution: Electron wrapper around existing Vite app

### Why Electron over PWA or Tauri
- PWA: still a browser tab, no file system access
- Tauri: requires Rust toolchain, complex setup
- Electron: wraps existing Vite app with minimal changes,
  gives file system access needed for save/load system,
  standard distribution format (exe/dmg/AppImage)

### Implementation steps
1. npm install --save-dev electron electron-builder
2. Add main.js — Electron entry point, creates BrowserWindow
   pointing to localhost:5173 (dev) or dist/index.html (prod)
3. Add electron:dev script: concurrently runs vite + electron
4. Add electron:build script: vite build + electron-builder
5. Configure electron-builder in package.json for
   win/mac/linux targets

### What this unlocks
- Save/load system (read/write JSON to file system)
- Native window chrome (title bar, minimize/maximize)
- Offline play
- Distributable .exe/.dmg/.AppImage

### Estimated effort
2-3 hours for basic setup.
Additional 2-4 hours for save/load integration.

## DoD
Developer can run: npm run electron:dev
Player gets a native window without opening a browser.
Save/load works via native file dialogs.
title: [FEAT] Electron desktop app — native game window with save/load

body:
## Problem
Revenue accrues every 16ms tick — feels like a meaningless
counter with no connection to actual network activity.
This was the intended design from the start: revenue should
be earned when data is successfully delivered, not passively.

## Design
When a packet session completes a full path traversal
(terminal → gateway), trigger a revenue event:
  revenue += deliveryValue(terminal.bandwidth, pathQuality)

Where pathQuality = average signal strength along the path.
Better paths = higher revenue per delivery.

## Why this is hard
The current simulation runs in a Web Worker with no direct
access to React state. Options to implement cleanly:

### Option A — Worker returns delivery events
SimulationWorker returns a deliveryEvents[] array alongside
the existing node/link data. Each event contains:
  { terminalId, revenue, pathQuality, timestamp }
useISPStore onmessage handler processes events and adds
revenue to money.
- Pro: clean separation, worker stays authoritative
- Pro: delivery events can drive floating +$ UI text
- Con: slightly more complex worker output

### Option B — Delivery counter in worker, revenue in store
Worker tracks delivery count per terminal per tick.
Store calculates revenue = deliveryCount * ratePerDelivery.
- Pro: simpler worker output
- Con: loses per-delivery granularity for UI feedback

### Option C — Interval-based billing (5 second batches)
Every 5 seconds, calculate revenue based on total data
delivered in that window. Show +$X floating text.
- Pro: low performance cost, feels alive
- Con: less directly tied to individual deliveries

## Recommendation
Option A — delivery events from worker. Enables the
most immersive experience including floating revenue text
and per-connection statistics.

## DoD
Revenue increases only when packets successfully complete
path traversal. Player can see +$X appear above terminals
on delivery. Idle network earns zero revenue.
title: [FEAT] Revenue per completed packet delivery — immersive billing

body:
## Problem
Map panning lags when 5+ nodes are active because the
simulation worker posts messages every 16ms, causing
React to re-render all nodes and links during drag.

This should be fixed before feature/map-system merges.

## Options

### Option A — Reduce tick rate during pan (recommended)
While isPanningRef is true, change tickRate to 100ms.
Restore to normal on pointerup.
- Pro: imperceptible freeze, major perf gain
- Pro: 1 line change in tick()
- Con: slight simulation inaccuracy during pan

### Option B — Pause simulation during pan
Skip worker.postMessage() while isPanningRef is true.
- Pro: zero rendering work during drag
- Con: nodes/packets visibly freeze on long pans

### Option C — CSS transform on game layer
Apply transform: scale() translate() to a SVG <g>
wrapper containing all game content. No viewBox changes
during pan at all.
- Pro: true 60fps regardless of node count
- Con: coordinate system for clicks needs reconciliation
  (previously attempted, reverted due to background misalignment)

## Target branch
feature/map-system — fix before PR merges to dev.

## DoD
Panning feels smooth at 10+ active nodes on a mid-range laptop.
title: [PERF] Map pan lag with active nodes — fix during feature/map-system

body:
## Context
The game needs different map detail at each range level.
The existing world-map.png handles global. A New York
state SVG is available for national/regional. OpenStreetMap
GeoJSON handles local street level.

## Map Layer Architecture

| Range | Level | Data | Detail |
|---|---|---|---|
| Local | 1 | OSM GeoJSON bbox | Streets ~2km² |
| Regional | 2 | City SVG e.g. NY boroughs | Districts |
| National | 3 | State SVG e.g. NY counties | Boundaries |
| Global | 4 | world-map.png | Continents |

## Tasks

### Phase 1 — NY SVG at National range
- Add NY county SVG to public/maps/ny_state.svg
- Render at rangeLevel 3-4 as dark-styled background
- Style: dark fill, subtle white/cyan border lines

### Phase 2 — OSM GeoJSON for Local streets
- Fetch streets for ~2km² bbox around DEFAULT_START
- Store as public/maps/cities/{city_id}_streets.geojson
- Render at rangeLevel 1, opacity 0.15-0.20
- Include only: highway=residential/primary/secondary/tertiary

### Phase 3 — Multi-city config
- Each city references svgMap and osmBbox in config
- Adding Tokyo/London/São Paulo = config + GeoJSON file only
- No code changes needed per city

## Coordinate system
All maps use existing latLonToXY() Mercator projection.
Node positions stored as lat/lon, converted at draw time.
Consistent across all range levels.

## DoD
- NY SVG renders at range 3-4 with game aesthetic
- OSM streets render at range 1
- New city = config entry + GeoJSON file, no code change
title: [FEAT] Multi-Layer Map System — OSM Streets + Regional SVG

body:
Currently packets route to random destinations. The intended design is that terminals represent actual customers who want to reach specific services (mainframes in 70s, web servers in 90s, CDNs in modern). Each terminal should have an assigned destination service node. Dijkstra finds the shortest path from terminal to that service. The player's job is to build a network that efficiently connects customers to services.

Tasks:
- Define service node types per era:
  70s: University Mainframe
  80s: Corporate Server
  90s: Web Server / ISP POP
  Modern: CDN Edge Node
- Each terminal spawned near a demand cell gets assigned the nearest service node as its destination
- activePaths tracks terminal → service path via Dijkstra
- Packet color/speed reflects path quality to that service
- Revenue scales with successful delivery rate to assigned service (not just any reachable node)
- UI: show destination assignment in node sidebar

DoD: A terminal's packet visibly travels from the customer premises to a specific service node. Disconnecting the path drops that terminal's revenue to zero.
title: [FEAT] Demand-Driven Packet Routing — Customer-to-Service Traffic

body:
Each combination of era + range level should have a distinct visual treatment:

  70s + Local: Green phosphor tint on streets, analog grid feel, no satellite imagery
  80s + Local: Slightly cleaner, IBM blue tints
  90s + Local: Color, more detail, early digital feel
  Modern + Local: Full color, high detail, Google Maps feel

Tasks:
- Apply era-specific CSS filters or SVG color transforms to the street layer based on currentEra
- 70s: SVG filter to desaturate + green tint the map
- Street opacity increases with era (more visible in modern era as data is richer)
- Building footprints: add in 90s+, not in 70s (in 70s you don't have digital building data)

DoD: Switching eras while in local view visibly changes the map aesthetic. The 70s feels like looking at a telephone company engineering diagram, the modern era feels like a live network operations center.
title: [FEAT] Local Map — Era-Specific Visual Theme per Range

body:
## Context
The network should feel alive and unpredictable. Random events create strategic pressure and narrative moments.

## Requirements
Requires: #125 (hardware failure system)
Blocks: nothing

## Tasks
- Add processEvents() to tick() with 60s cooldown between event checks and 10% base trigger probability
- Add activeEvents: Event[] to store state
- Implement these event types:

  CABLE_CUT (70s+):
    Selects a random active link, reduces its bandwidth to 10% for 120 seconds or until player pays repair.
  
  TRAFFIC_SPIKE (80s+):
    Selects a high-traffic node, multiplies traffic by 5x for 60 seconds (Slashdot effect).
  
  SOLAR_FLARE (modern only):
    Global latency multiplier ×3 for 30 seconds.
    Revenue reduced 30% during event.

- UI: Active incidents shown as red alert banner above log panel with countdown timer

## Definition of Done
At least one event triggers within 10 minutes of gameplay. Each event type has correct era gating.
title: [FEAT] Living World — Random Incident System

body:
## Context
Currently latency is only a Dijkstra weight. Physical signal propagation at the speed of light in copper (~200,000 km/sec) should create minimum latency floors based on real distance. In the 70s this was measurable and frustrating for users.

## Requirements
Requires: #122 (kbps scale)
Blocks: nothing

## Tasks
- Add propagation delay to each link: delay_ms = (distance_km / 200000) × 1000
- In SimulationWorker, add this to the Dijkstra edge weight as a floor (cannot be reduced by upgrades)
- Fiber increases propagation speed to ~200,000 km/sec (same, glass is similar to copper for this)
- Satellite links (future): 600ms floor (geostationary orbit round trip)
- Display path latency in the node sidebar

## Definition of Done
A cross-country link in the 70s shows measurable latency that cannot be optimized away, only routed around.
title: [FEAT] Signal Propagation Delay

body:
## Context
In the 70s, protocol overhead consumed 30-40% of raw bandwidth. Effective throughput was ~60-70% of link speed. As protocols improve (TCP/IP in 80s, optimized stacks in 90s+), overhead decreases.

## Requirements
Requires: #122 (kbps scale)
Blocks: nothing

## Tasks
- Add protocolEfficiency: number to eraConfig.json (70s: 0.60, 80s: 0.72, 90s: 0.85, 00s+: 0.95)
- In SimulationWorker, multiply effective bandwidth by era.modifiers.protocolEfficiency before traffic calc
- This naturally makes 70s networks feel sluggish and rewards era advancement even without tech upgrades

## Definition of Done
A 56kbps terminal in the 70s effectively delivers ~33kbps. Same terminal in the 90s delivers ~47kbps.
title: [FEAT] Protocol Overhead — Effective Throughput

body:
## Context

Replace placeholder icons with actual tech card artwork. This is purely visual — no logic changes.

## Task

- Define asset naming convention: `/public/assets/techs/{tech_id}.png`
- Add `iconRef?: string` to `NodeRegistryEntry` and `TechEntry` schemas
- Load images in `TechTree.tsx` card component with fallback to colored icon if asset missing
- Add hover tooltip with full description and modifier details
- Visual polish:
  - Unlock animation on successful research
  - Connection line glow on unlock
  - Era section headers with period-appropriate styling

## Acceptance Criteria

- Missing images degrade gracefully to placeholder icon
- Tooltips show all relevant tech data on hover
- Unlock animation plays on successful research
- Tree remains fully functional if all images are missing

## Dev Tool Requirement

N/A

## Out of Scope

- Creating the actual artwork (separate design task)
title: [UI] Tech Tree — Card Artwork & Visual Polish

body:
## Context

Players need a visual interface to browse and unlock technologies, similar in concept to World of Tanks' tech tree — cards connected by dependency lines, showing locked/unlocked/available states.

## Task

- Create `src/components/TechTree.tsx` as a fullscreen overlay or dedicated panel (decide based on existing UI layout)
- Each tech renders as a card showing: `name`, `description`, `tpCost`, `requires` badges, `scope` indicators, lock/unlock state
- Dependency lines connect cards based on `requires[]` relationships
- Three visual states per card:
  - **LOCKED** — prerequisites not met (greyed, no interaction)
  - **AVAILABLE** — prerequisites met, TP sufficient (highlighted)
  - **UNLOCKED** — already researched (fully lit)
- Era sections divide the tree visually (70s row, 80s row, etc.)
- Clicking an AVAILABLE card calls `unlockTech(id)`
- `position: {x, y}` in `techTreeConfig.json` drives card placement

## Acceptance Criteria

- All 8 techs render in correct positions with correct states
- Dependency lines are visible and update when techs unlock
- Cards react correctly to TP changes and unlock events
- No placeholder images required — icons or colored shapes acceptable
- Responsive to era changes (80s section greyed until era advances)

## Dev Tool Requirement

N/A

## Out of Scope

- Tech artwork and images (Issue 6 — [UI] Tech Tree — Card Artwork & Visual Polish)
- Animations and polish
title: [UI] Tech Tree Interface — Layout & Card Components

body:
## [DATA/FEATURE] Implementation of World City Registry & Landing Station Logic

O objetivo desta issue é expandir a camada de rede do ISP Simulator para incluir roteamento global realista. A meta é evoluir de nós abstratos para um registro geolocalizado utilizando uma arquitetura React + Zustand.

Closes: #N/A
Labels: feat, data, P1-high

### 🛠️ Especificações Técnicas

1. **Arquitetura de Dados & State Management**
   - **Data Provider**: Criar um arquivo `cities.json` (ou constante) com aprox. 200 hubs globais. Schema: `id`, `name`, `country`, `continent`, `lat`, `lon`, `isLandingStation` (boolean).
   - **State Manager**: `useISPStore.ts`. Adicionar um seletor para filtrar nós onde `isLandingStation` seja true.
   - **Logic Hooks**: 
     - Função `canConnect(nodeA, nodeB)`: Se os continentes forem diferentes, requerer que ambos sejam Landing Stations.
     - Utilizar o Teorema/Fórmula de Haversine para cálculo de latência/distância física de forma a aplicar métricas realistas ao `tick()`.

2. **Requisitos de UI/UX**
   - **Estilo (CSS/Tailwind)**: Diferenciação visual para Landing Stations no mapa global (cores/classes baseadas no estado).
   - **Feedback Visual**: `NetworkNode.tsx` deve renderizar brilhos específicos (glow effect) ou marcações para evidenciar portas de entrada transcontinentais.
   - **Performance**: Opportune a verificação de links na árvore DOM do React para evitar "engine lag" no render cycle (60 FPS).

3. **Invariantes Lógicos**
   - [X] As fórmulas de rede devem respeitar `ARCHITECTURE.md`.
   - [X] Nenhuma lógica de negócio deve vazar para componentes React.

### ⛓️ Coordenação de Dependências

- **Dependência Crítica**: Concluída a transição do mapa global vs. local (Isso escala o `WorldMapView`).
- **Sincronização Visual**: `LocalMapView` e `WorldMapView` para links intercontinentais.

---

### ✅ Definição de Pronto (DoD)

- [ ] Arquivo `cities.json` estruturado e carregado pelo `useISPStore`.
- [ ] Lógica matemática (`canConnect` e Haversine) isolada no Zustand Store.
- [ ] UI respeita o sistema de temas dinâmicos (Tailwind) com destaque para Landing Stations.
- [ ] Teste de estresse: O sistema não quebra renderizando 200 cidades com múltiplas rotas ativas continentais e intercontinentais.
- [ ] Documentação atualizada no `/docs` ou `agent-instructions.md`.

---

**Hipótese:** A restrição rígida de conexão intercontinental em *Landing Stations* obrigará o jogador a criar "backbones" estratégicos (Tier-1) dentro de seu continente antes de tentar expansões globais oceânicas (como Fortaleza <-> Marseille).
title: [DATA/FEATURE] Implementation of World City Registry & Landing Station Logic

body:
## Context
The Last Mile refers to the final leg of infrastructure
connecting the local hub to the customer premises.
This is the core gameplay of the Local range level.

## Tasks
- Implement trenching cost: cost = distance × terrainModifier
- Era hardware: 70s copper drop cables vs modern fiber-to-home
- Signal integrity: links beyond tech maxDistance spike packet
  loss immediately, forcing the player to use repeaters/hubs
- Customer connection: terminals placed near demand cells
  generate revenue based on signal quality at that cell

## Dependencies
- Requires: #86 (Demand Cell Heatmap — need demand zones first)
- Requires: Multi-Layer Map System (need streets to trench along)
- Blocks: core local gameplay loop

## DoD
A player in local range can lay cable from a hub to a
terminal along a street, pay trenching cost, and earn
revenue from that customer connection.
title: [FEAT] Local Construction Mechanics (The Last Mile)

body:
## 📖 Contexto e "User Story"
**O que:** Creating the "Content" for the local map.
**Por que (Experiência do Jogador):** Blocker for Issue 3. (You can't "Trench" if there's no demand to reach).

---

## 🛠️ Escopo e Arquitetura
- **The Implementation:**
  - Instead of an empty map, generate a 2D grid of Demand Cells.
  - Each cell has a density value (Residential, Commercial, Industrial).
  - Revenue is calculated by the node's proximity to these cells (The "Coverage" mechanic).

---

## 🚦 Dependency Map
- **Blocker (Depends on...):** Hierarchical State (Issue 1)
- **Unlocks (Base for...):** Profitable Local Strategy.
title: [LOGIC] The "Demand Cell" Heatmap Engine

body:
### Descrição
Implementar suporte para múltiplos cenários iniciais (Presets) baseados em topografias reais, permitindo que o jogador escolha uma "Cidade Sede" ao iniciar um novo jogo. O sistema deve utilizar uma renderização geográfica em camadas para transicionar detalhes conforme o nível de zoom (Range System).

### Especificações Técnicas

1. **Data Provider (Geo-Presets)**:
   - Integrar arquivos JSON de geometria real (ex: `public/maps/sf.json`, `public/maps/tokyo.json`).
   - Cada preset deve definir as coordenadas `x, y` iniciais do **Core Gateway (ID: '0')** específicas para aquela malha urbana.

2. **Geographical Layer Toggle**:
   O `LogisticMap` deve renderizar três camadas distintas de SVG:
   - **Layer_Base**: Continentes e oceanos (Sempre visível em todos os ranges).
   - **Layer_Infrastructure**: Principais rodovias e divisas nacionais (Visível apenas em `range < National`).
   - **Layer_Detail**: Malha urbana detalhada, distritos e topografia de vizinhança (Visível apenas em `range === Local`).

3. **Preset Selector (Main Menu)**:
   - Criar uma tela de "New Game" ou Modal de Seleção de Região.
   - O estado do `useISPStore` deve ser resetado e re-inicializado com o preset escolhido (Economia inicial, Nome da Região e Posição do Core).

### Coordenação de Dependências

- **Dependência Crítica**: Esta issue depende da **Issue #60 (Snap ViewBox)**, pois a navegação discreta deve ser limitada à área geográfica do Preset selecionado para evitar que o jogador "saia do mapa".
- **Sincronização Visual**: O Sistema de Eras deve aplicar filtros (CRT, Retro, Modern) sobre as camadas geográficas específicas de cada região sem perda de nitidez.
- **Qualidade Visual**: Os vetores SVG devem ser simplificados para performance, mas manter a silhueta reconhecível das cidades (ex: Golden Gate em SF, Baía de Tóquio).

### Definição de Pronto (DoD)
- [ ] Menu de seleção de região funcional no estado inicial do app.
- [ ] Carregamento dinâmico de coordenadas e malha SVG baseado no Preset selecionado.
- [ ] Otimização de camadas de detalhe (ocultar `Layer_Detail` no zoom Global para manter 60fps).
- [ ] Estabilidade de renderização: Os links não podem "flutuar" fora das posições ao trocar de preset.

>**Hipótese**: Um sistema de presets regionais aumenta o replay value e permite testar o balanceamento da economia em diferentes densidades urbanas (ex: Tokyo compacta vs. San Francisco espalhada).
title: [FEAT] Multi-Region Preset System & Layered SVG Maps

body:
## 📖 Context & Purpose
Need to verify copyright and usage licenses for all themes, maps, and assets (fonts, SVGs) used in the game. This avoids future legal liabilities and ensures we can monetize or safely distribute the game.

## 📋 Checklist
- [ ] Verify terms of `world-map.png` SVG/Asset.
- [ ] Ensure `useISPStore` logic implementations (like BFS algorithms) do not copy restricted GPL code without compliance.
- [ ] Verify standard Windows 95 UI styling usages.
- [ ] Document what we can legally use and attribution requirements.

**Note**: Important Dev Tool priority, establishing safe baseline for distribution.
title: docs: [LEGAL] Copyright and Asset Usage Verification

body:
## 📖 Contexto e Visão
Para aumentar o LTV (Lifetime Value) e a rejogabilidade, o sistema deve suportar "Cenários" que substituem as regras padrão do jogo. O primeiro cenário de demonstração (e potencial DLC) será a **"Guerra Fria: Operação Arpanet vs. Globus"**.

O que muda: O jogador escolhe um lado (EUA ou URSS). A interface, o idioma e os objetivos mudam drasticamente. Em vez de lucro puro, o objetivo é **Dominância de Dados** e **Resiliência contra Sabotagem**.

## 🛠️ Arquitetura Técnica (The "Plugin" Model)
Para que os cenários sejam modulares, o desenvolvimento deve implementar:
- **State Overrides**: O arquivo do cenário deve conseguir sobrescrever variáveis do `useISPStore` (ex: `currencySymbol: "SUR"`, `baseLatency: 800ms`).
- **Asset Swapping**: Sistema de injeção de temas que carrega um CSS/Tailwind config específico para o cenário (Interface verde-fóssil da CIA vs. Terminal Cirílico da KGB).
- **Event Pool**: Cada cenário tem seu próprio banco de dados de eventos aleatórios (ex: "Cabo cortado por submarino", "Espião infiltrado no Data Center").

## 📈 Especificações do Cenário: "Cold War"
- **Mechanic - Jamming**: Hackers inimigos podem "embaralhar" o sinal de um nó. O jogador precisa gastar *Intelligence Points* para limpar a linha.
- **Language/UI**: Tradução completa da UI baseada no lado escolhido. Se escolher URSS, os logs devem usar caracteres cirílicos e terminologia soviética.
- **Victory Condition**: Conectar 80% das capitais do bloco aliado antes do "Relógio do Apocalipse" atingir meia-noite.

## ✅ Critérios de Aceite (DoD)
- [ ] **Scenario Loader**: O jogo deve carregar um cenário a partir de um arquivo .json externo sem crashar.
- [ ] **UI Abstraction**: Todos os textos da UI devem vir de um i18n (dicionário) que o cenário pode substituir.
- [ ] **Monetization Hook**: Implementação de um check de `isUnlocked` para permitir o bloqueio de cenários premium.
- [ ] **Save Isolation**: Saves de cenários não podem corromper o save do "Modo Carreira" padrão.

## 💰 Nota de Monetização
Este sistema permite vender "Scenario Packs":
- **Grátis**: "O Nascimento da Web (Anos 90)".
- **Premium 1**: "Guerra Fria (Espionagem e Sabotagem)".
- **Premium 2**: "Marte 2050 (Latência Extrema de 20 minutos por pacote)".

## 🤖 Agentic Instructions
Não use `if/else` espalhados pelo código para checar o cenário. Use o **Strategy Pattern**. Crie uma interface `GameRules` e injete a lógica do cenário nela durante a inicialização do Store.
title: [EPIC][FEAT] Implementation of Scenario Engine & 'Cyber-Iron Curtain' Expansion

body:
### Description
The current node interaction lacks a sense of directional data flow and physical signal boundaries.

### Technical Requirements
- **Signal Range Visualization**:
  - When a node is selected, render a subtle radial signal range circle.
  - Use a radial gradient and a sutil "breathing" animation (`animate-pulse` or similar).
- **Directional Particles**:
  - Replace the "back and forth" node vibration with directional data particles.
  - Render small SVG points that travel along established links from source to target.
  - Densitity of particles should scale linearly with the `Traffic` state of the link.

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Signal range is only visible on selection or in linking mode.
- [ ] Particles move in a single direction (source -> target).
- [ ] High-traffic nodes are visually distinct due to higher particle density.

**Labels**: `ux`, `vfx`, `immersion`
title: [UX] Signal Pulse & Range Visualization

body:
### Description
Network links currently look like "lasers" that ignore geography. We will refactor the link rendering to follow realistic paths, simulating terrestrial cables following infrastructures and undersea cables avoiding landmasses.

### Technical Requirements
- **`LinkRenderer` Refactor**:
  - **Terrestrial Links**: Use jitter or Simplex noise to make paths follow irregular terrestrial routes (simulating highway/road paths).
  - **Submarine Links**: Implement specific "Coastal Entry/Exit" coordinates. Cables must curve around landmasses rather than crossing them.
- **Pulse Animation**: Add `stroke-dasharray` animations where the pulse speed is inversely proportional to length/latency.

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Links no longer pass through land incorrectly (except core backbones).
- [ ] Terrestrial paths have a "jagged" but organic feel compared to straight lines.
- [ ] Long-distance links show slower data pulses to signal physical latency.
- [ ] Links update dynamically as nodes are added or moved.
title: [LOGIC] Dynamic Routing & Pathfinding (Realistic Links)

body:
### Description
Exploration and expansion are key gameplay pillars. We will implement an infrastructure-based "Fog of War" that obscures areas where the player currently has no presence, requiring strategic expansion to reveal the map.

### Technical Requirements
- **Coverage Calculation**: Add `coverageRadius` to `ISPNode` and track it in `useISPStore`.
- **SVG Masking**: Use an SVG `<mask/>` to create the fog effect. Active nodes with signal should "cut holes" in the mask.
- **Fog Shaders**: implement a darkened/blurred overlay for areas outside coverage.
- **Expansion Logic**: Allow players to see and click on potential expansion points only if they are within (or just on the edge of) the current coverage range.

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Areas without network presence are visibly darkened/Fogged.
- [ ] Fog correctly clears in a radius around newly activated nodes.
- [ ] Fog of War is visually pleasing (smooth gradients, not sharp edges).
- [ ] "Buy coverage" mechanic effectively interactive.
title: [FEAT] Proximity-Based Fog of War

body:
### Description
Generic green circles don't convey the physical reality of an ISP. We will replace them with era-specific hardware icons and real-time status indicators that reflect the physical health of the network.

### Technical Requirements
- **`MapNode` Asset Evolution**:
  - **70s**: `<ModemIcon/>` (Analog/Mainframe style).
  - **90s**: `<TowerIcon/>` (Broadband tower style).
  - **Modern**: `<DataCenterIcon/>` (Cloud/Server rack style).
- **Real-time Status Labels**: Add labels next to nodes displaying:
  - Node Name (e.g., `SFO-01`).
  - Real-time Latency (ms).
  - Current Load (%).
- **Particle/Visual Effects**: Implement SVG-based particles to simulate smoke/faíscas when a node hits >95% load.

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Node icons update automatically upon era change.
- [ ] Status labels are legible at all zoom levels.
- [ ] Overload visual effects trigger correctly during traffic spikes.
- [ ] Hardware assets are optimized for SVGs to maintain performance.
title: [UX] Physical Node Representation (Realistic Assets)

body:
### Description
The dashboard needs a sense of time and urgency. A historical news ticker will provide context on the current era while serving as a high-priority notification system for network incidents.

### Technical Requirements
- **Ticker UI**: A high-z-index sliding text component at the top of the HUD.
- **Era Context**:
  - **70s**: "ARPANET expands to Hawaii", "First email sent by Ray Tomlinson".
  - **90s**: "AOL reaches 10 million users", "Google launches in a garage".
  - **Modern**: "Global fiber demand surges", "4K streaming becomes industry standard".
- **Incident Override**: When an event from Issue #2 is active, the ticker must switch to a red `'BREAKING NEWS'` mode with high-contrast text.
- **Performance**: Use CSS `transform: translateX` for the marquee effect to ensure smooth performance.

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Ticker correctly cycles through era-specific headlines.
- [ ] Ticker automatically updates when `currentEra` changes.
- [ ] Breaking news override takes immediate priority over casual headlines.
- [ ] Ticker text is legible against all `ScreenOverlay` shaders.
title: feat: Historical News Ticker & Incident Alerts

body:
### Description
Sound is a critical dimension of the ISP experience. We need an "audio landscape" that reacts to network health, making the player *feel* the weight of the data they are transporting.

### Technical Requirements
- **Data Hum**: Implement a looping low-frequency hum (Web Audio API or HTML5 Audio). Pitch and volume should be mapped to `totalDataTraffic` in the store.
- **Modem Handshake**: Trigger a high-fidelity 56k handshake sound effect specifically when `connectNodes` is called during the `'90s'` era.
- **Saturation Alarms**: A pulsing industrial alarm should trigger if any node's `traffic >= bandwidth`.
- **Audio Service**: Create a dedicated hook or singleton to manage audio state and prevent overlapping samples.

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Ambient hum volume scales linearly with network load.
- [ ] 56k sound plays only in the 90s era upon link creation.
- [ ] Alarm sounds only trigger during saturation events.
- [ ] Mute toggle added to the `TopBar`.
title: ux: Procedural Audio Feedback Loop

body:
### Description
The simulation currently feels too predictable. A "Living World" event system will introduce random technical and environmental challenges that force the player to adapt their network topology and financial strategy.

### Technical Requirements
- **Event Engine in `useISPStore`**:
  - Implement a `processEvents` function in the `tick` loop.
  - 60s cooldown between potential event triggers.
  - 10% base probability per check.
- **Event Variations**:
  - `Undersea Shark` (90s+): Randomly selects an `ISPLink` of type `cable` and sets bandwidth to 10% for 2 mins (or until paid repair).
  - `Slashdot Effect`: Randomly spikes traffic on a high-tier node by 500% for 60s.
  - `Solar Flares` (Modern): Global latency increase. Implementation: total revenue decreased by 30% for 30s as "Data Retransmission" costs rise.
- **State Management**: New `activeEvents` array in Zustand store to track active modifiers.

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] System successfully triggers at least one random event within 10 minutes of gameplay.
- [ ] Shark attacks correctly throttle cable bandwidth.
- [ ] Slashdot effect causes node saturation and triggers critical logs.
- [ ] UI reflects active incidents (e.g., red alerts).
title: feat: Living World - Random Incident System
